# UC-17.2.7 · VPN Bandwidth Consumption

## Description

Per-user bandwidth tracking identifies heavy users, guides capacity planning, and detects potential data exfiltration.

## Value

Per-user bandwidth tracking identifies heavy users, guides capacity planning, and detects potential data exfiltration.

## Implementation

Track VPN session byte counters per user. Alert on users with excessive upload (potential data exfiltration). Report on bandwidth distribution for capacity planning. Identify optimization opportunities (video offload, split-tunnel).

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_cisco-asa`, `Splunk_TA_paloalto` (GlobalProtect), `TA-fortinet_fortigate`, `Splunk_TA_juniper`, RADIUS accounting.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: VPN session accounting (bytes in/out).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Track VPN session byte counters per user. Alert on users with excessive upload (potential data exfiltration). Report on bandwidth distribution for capacity planning. Identify optimization opportunities (video offload, split-tunnel).

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=vpn sourcetype="cisco:asa"
| stats sum(bytes_in) as bytes_in, sum(bytes_out) as bytes_out by user
| eval total_gb=round((bytes_in+bytes_out)/1073741824,2)
| sort -total_gb
| head 20
```

Understanding this SPL

**VPN Bandwidth Consumption** — Per-user bandwidth tracking identifies heavy users, guides capacity planning, and detects potential data exfiltration.

Documented **Data sources**: VPN session accounting (bytes in/out). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_cisco-asa`, `Splunk_TA_paloalto` (GlobalProtect), `TA-fortinet_fortigate`, `Splunk_TA_juniper`, RADIUS accounting. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: vpn; **sourcetype**: cisco:asa. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=vpn, sourcetype="cisco:asa". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by user** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **total_gb** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
• Limits the number of rows with `head`.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with Palo Alto Networks Prisma Access or firewall manager for the same users, rules, and time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Step 4 — Operationalize
Step 4 — Operationalize
Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (bandwidth by user), Pie chart (upload vs download), Line chart (total bandwidth trend), Table (top bandwidth consumers).

## SPL

```spl
index=vpn sourcetype="cisco:asa"
| stats sum(bytes_in) as bytes_in, sum(bytes_out) as bytes_out by user
| eval total_gb=round((bytes_in+bytes_out)/1073741824,2)
| sort -total_gb
| head 20
```

## CIM SPL

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.user, All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest span=1h
| sort - count
```

## Visualization

Bar chart (bandwidth by user), Pie chart (upload vs download), Line chart (total bandwidth trend), Table (top bandwidth consumers).
